metrics.narrative: No quantified business case has been captured. The Next_Step__c log (13 May 2026) records the in-person summit meeting and upcoming workshop but does not articulate the buyer's pain, savings target, cost reduction, cycle-time improvement, or ROI expectation. The product scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin Intake) is broad and signals a full S2P transformation, yet no procurement spend baseline, contract-leakage estimate, or efficiency metric has been documented. The half-day workshop scheduled for Q3 is the natural venue to quantify the value case—procurement cycle time, maverick spend reduction, supplier consolidation savings, and invoice-processing cost per transaction—and tie Zycus capabilities to Radicare's P&L impact. Until that session, metrics remain a knowledge gap.

sources: Avoma manifest: introductory meeting 11 May 2026 (no recording)
champion.narrative: Dr. Hamidey Hamid (Group Chief Procurement and Vendor Development Officer) is the single identified buyer contact and is developing as a potential champion. He met George John at the 4th ASEAN Procurement Innovation Summit on 13 May 2026, visited the Zycus booth, expressed plans to attend Horizon, and committed to a half-day workshop (source: Next_Step__c 13 May 2026; OpportunityContactRole Decision Maker). This level of engagement—summit attendance, booth visit, Horizon interest, and workshop commitment—signals genuine interest and procurement-process ownership. However, champion strength cannot yet be rated as strong because (a) there is no evidence of active advocacy (no internal introductions to CFO/COO, no competitive intelligence shared, no draft RFP shared early), (b) access to the Economic Buyer is unconfirmed, and (c) his engagement is recent (6 weeks) with no second touch logged since the summit. The workshop will test champion development: does he bring the broader committee, share the draft RFP, position Zycus favorably in the evaluation, and facilitate the CFO introduction. Until then, he is a willing procurement partner, not yet a coach or advocate.

competition.narrative: Oracle is the sole named competitor (source: Competitors__c in Salesforce). No call transcript, task note, or Next_Step entry elaborates on the competitive context—whether Oracle means Oracle Procurement Cloud (the direct S2P peer), Oracle ERP Cloud (the incumbent ERP with procurement modules the buyer may be considering), or Oracle as a brand shorthand for a broader set of alternatives. The threat level is medium by default (named competitor, strong brand, likely incumbent ERP if Radicare runs Oracle financials), but the actual competitive posture—are they in the RFP, are they the incumbent being displaced, what is their pricing and fit weakness—is unknown. The upcoming workshop and RFP round in Q3 will surface the full shortlist, Oracle's role (incumbent vs. challenger), and the wedge Zycus must exploit (likely Merlin AI intake automation, procurement-specific depth vs. ERP add-on modules, and lower TCO). Until then, competitive intelligence is thin.

identify_pain.narrative: Specific operational or financial pain has not been articulated. The product scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin Intake) implies a full S2P transformation, suggesting dissatisfaction with the current procurement stack or manual processes, but no buyer quote, task note, or Next_Step entry names the pain. Common pain points for a healthcare procurement organization of Radicare's scale include fragmented supplier data, slow contract approval cycles, maverick spend, invoice-processing backlog, and lack of procurement visibility for finance, yet none of these have been confirmed. The upcoming workshop is the venue to surface and document the pain—what is broken today, what is the cost of inaction, who owns the pain (procurement ops, finance, supply chain), and what procurement KPIs are under pressure—so the value case can be quantified and the pain owner can become the internal champion.

sources: Account contact: Nani Tajuddin (Group Director, Secretarial & Legal)
paper_process.narrative: Paper process and contracting mechanics are unknown. No MSA, SOW, legal review, InfoSec questionnaire, or procurement-channel detail has been documented. The RFP round expected in Q3 2026 (per Next_Step__c 13 May) will likely trigger the formal contracting path, but until then the buyer's legal, InfoSec, and procurement-operations requirements are a black box. The account contact list includes Nani Tajuddin (Group Director, Secretarial & Legal), signaling in-house legal capability, yet she has not been engaged on this opportunity. The next move is to surface the paper process during the workshop—RFP-to-contract timeline, legal review SLA, InfoSec requirements (data residency for Malaysia, SOC2/ISO27001 expectations), and any parent-company approval if Radicare is part of a larger group—so contracting does not stall post-selection.

economic_buyer.narrative: The Economic Buyer—who controls budget and commercial sign-off—is not yet identified. Dr. Hamidey Hamid (Group Chief Procurement and Vendor Development Officer, source: OpportunityContactRole Decision Maker, plus summit meeting 13 May 2026) is confirmed as the Decision Maker and procurement process owner, but budget authority for a $200k S2P platform typically sits one level up (CFO, COO, or C-suite sponsor). The account-level contact list includes Hidaya Yahaya (Chief Operating Officer & Chief Finance Officer), who is the likely Economic Buyer given her dual COO/CFO role, but she has not appeared on this opportunity's contact roles, in any task, or in the single recorded touchpoint (11 May introductory meeting, no transcript). The next move is to map the approval chain explicitly during the upcoming workshop and secure a direct introduction to the CFO/COO, confirming budget ownership and sign-off authority before the RFP round in Q3.

sources: Account contact list (CFO/COO/legal/procurement roles present but not yet engaged on this opp)
decision_process.narrative: The decision process is partially visible. The Next_Step__c log (13 May 2026) outlines the near-term path: in-person summit meeting completed 13 May, Horizon event attendance planned, half-day workshop to be scheduled soon, and RFP expected Q3 2026. This maps to a formal procurement cycle (discovery → workshop → RFP → shortlist → selection). However, the approval chain and committee composition are unknown. Dr. Hamidey Hamid (Group Chief Procurement and Vendor Development Officer) is the confirmed Decision Maker and likely chairs the evaluation, but the CFO/COO sign-off layer, legal/InfoSec review gates, and any steering-committee structure have not been surfaced. The single-threaded contact coverage (one buyer-side contact role) means the broader committee—finance, IT, operations, legal—is unmapped. The workshop is the opportunity to clarify the full approval chain, timeline from RFP to contract, and who joins each evaluation stage.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria have not been documented. The RFP is expected in Q3 2026 (per Next_Step__c 13 May), which will formalize the buyer's evaluation framework, yet no requirements document, scoring rubric, or stated priorities have been captured. The product scope (full S2P suite including Merlin Intake) implies the buyer values end-to-end integration, AI-augmented intake, and supplier-management depth, but these are inferences, not confirmed criteria. The upcoming half-day workshop is the critical venue to surface and lock in the decision criteria—procurement cycle time, user adoption, ERP integration (likely SAP or Oracle ERP given the Oracle competitor flag), AI/Merlin fit for requisition automation, and total cost of ownership—before the RFP formalizes the scorecard. Without this workshop output, criteria remain a knowledge gap.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AIS (AI excitement) score, status, or rationale has been captured in Salesforce (AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c all null). The product scope includes Merlin Intake (AI-augmented requisition automation), which is a strong signal that the buyer values AI capabilities and the deal is positioned around Zycus AI strengths. However, no call transcript or buyer feedback validates AI appetite, and no documented pain (e.g. slow requisition approval, poor spend visibility, manual procurement workflows) directly ties to the AI-automation value prop. The AI fit signal is inferred from product scope, not confirmed by buyer enthusiasm or stated AI requirements. The workshop is the critical venue to validate AI appetite—demonstrate Merlin Intake live, surface the buyer's AI maturity and readiness, and lock in AI-native intake automation as a decision criterion before Oracle frames the RFP as an ERP-bundle play. Until then, AI fit is a hypothesis.

items.detail: Economic Buyer (CFO/COO) is unmapped; budget authority and C-level sponsorship unconfirmed. Dr. Hamidey (Decision Maker, procurement process owner) is the single buyer-side contact role; Hidaya Yahaya (COO & CFO per account contact list) is the likely Economic Buyer but has not been engaged on this opportunity. A $200k S2P platform investment typically requires CFO/COO sign-off; without executive alignment, the deal can stall post-RFP at budget approval. The workshop is the opportunity to meet the CFO/COO, validate budget authority, and secure C-level sponsorship before the RFP formalizes the evaluation.

Single-threaded risk: only one buyer-side contact role (Decision Maker); no multi-thread redundancy across finance, IT, operations, legal. If the Decision Maker changes role, goes silent, or cannot carry the business case to the Economic Buyer, the deal is at risk. The account contact list shows high-value expansion targets (CFO/COO, legal, senior procurement leadership), but none are engaged on this opportunity. The workshop is the venue to bring the broader evaluation committee into the room and build redundancy.

Competitive intelligence on Oracle is thin; no insight on Oracle's role (incumbent ERP vs. shortlist challenger), pricing, buyer sentiment, or Oracle-specific weakness. Oracle is a credible enterprise brand and likely the incumbent ERP if Radicare runs Oracle financials; without competitive intelligence, Zycus cannot position the wedge (Merlin AI, procurement-specific depth, lower TCO) or lock in decision criteria that favor Zycus before Oracle frames the RFP. The workshop and pre-RFP discovery calls are the venues to surface the competitive landscape and build the Oracle counterstrategy.

Workshop timing is vague ('to be set-up soon' per Next_Step__c 13 May 2026, now ~6 weeks ago); RFP expected Q3 2026 (Jul-Sep, 1-9 weeks away). If the workshop slips into late Q3 or the RFP launches before the workshop, Zycus loses the opportunity to surface decision criteria, quantify the business case, and position strengths before the RFP formalizes the evaluation. The next move is to lock the workshop date within the next 14 days and run it before the RFP drops.

items.expected_effect: Clarifies whether Oracle is the incumbent ERP, a shortlist challenger, or both; surfaces the buyer's Oracle pricing and fit experience; positions Zycus strengths (Merlin Intake AI layer Oracle lacks, iContract advanced CLM vs. Oracle's basic contract repository, healthcare-sector references, faster time-to-value, lower TCO) and locks in decision criteria that favor Zycus before Oracle frames the RFP as an ERP-bundle play. Turns a named but uncharacterized competitor into a mapped threat with a clear wedge.

items.expected_effect: Builds multi-thread redundancy and reduces single-threaded risk; ensures finance (ROI validation), IT (ERP integration), and legal (contract review) are mapped early so the deal can progress post-RFP without stalls at approval gates. Corrects the data-hygiene gap (1 buyer-side contact role, below multi-thread bar) and improves deal visibility.

confidence_signals.summary: Early-stage deal with positive initial engagement (summit meeting 13 May, booth visit, Horizon interest, workshop commitment) but thin evidence base (no recorded call transcript, single buyer contact, no quantified business case, minimal competitive intelligence). Recent activity (LastActivityDate 13 Jun, 13 days ago) confirms the deal is live, not cooling. Confidence is medium: the engagement is real and forward-moving, but MEDDPICC coverage is partial (Economic Buyer, metrics, decision criteria, champion advocacy, competitive intelligence all gaps). The workshop in Q3 is the critical venue to upgrade confidence by surfacing the missing elements.

Next_Step__c shows in-person summit meeting 13 May (~6 weeks ago), Horizon attendance planned, workshop to be scheduled soon, and RFP expected Q3 2026. Pace is consistent with a 12-15 month enterprise motion (currently in discovery/formal-eval, aiming for RFP round Jul-Sep). No overdue milestones; the deal is on the planned path.

north_star_verdict.headline: Early-stage APAC deal progressing normally at 187 days to close; recent activity 13 days ago, upcoming workshop, and Q3 RFP timeline align with 12-15 month cycle. Oracle competitor noted; EB/champion gaps are typical for Qualified stage.

best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage partner-led APAC deal with solid initial engagement (summit meeting, booth visit, Horizon interest, workshop commitment) but thin multi-thread coverage (single Decision Maker contact, Economic Buyer unmapped), no quantified value case, and minimal competitive intelligence on Oracle. The upcoming half-day workshop in Q3 is the critical venue to surface decision criteria, map the approval chain, introduce the CFO/COO Economic Buyer, quantify the business case, and lock in champion advocacy before the RFP formalizes the evaluation. Three high-leverage best practices follow.

competitive_position.summary: Oracle is the sole named competitor (source: Competitors__c). Competitive context is thin—no call transcript or task elaborates whether Oracle means Oracle Procurement Cloud (direct S2P peer), Oracle ERP Cloud (incumbent ERP with procurement modules), or a placeholder. Threat level is medium by default: Oracle is a credible enterprise brand, likely the incumbent ERP if Radicare runs Oracle financials, and a viable procurement alternative. However, no pricing, RFP shortlist, buyer sentiment, or Oracle-specific weakness has been documented. The RFP round in Q3 2026 will surface the full competitive field and Oracle's actual role (incumbent being displaced vs. shortlist challenger). The Zycus wedge is likely Merlin AI intake automation (Merlin Intake is in scope), procurement-specific depth vs. ERP add-on modules, faster time-to-value, and lower TCO. Until the workshop and RFP, Oracle is a known but uncharacterized threat.

competitors.name: Oracle
competitors.how_we_win: Position Merlin Intake as the AI-native requisition automation layer Oracle ERP lacks; emphasize procurement-specific depth (iContract advanced CLM, iSupplier collaboration, iSource strategic sourcing) vs. Oracle's ERP-add-on procurement modules; prove faster time-to-value and lower TCO with healthcare-sector references; lock in decision criteria around AI, user experience, and S2P completeness before Oracle frames the RFP as an ERP-bundle play.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin Intake is in the product scope (source: Products__c), signaling the buyer values AI-augmented requisition automation and the deal is positioned around Zycus AI capabilities. However, no AIS score, AIS status, or buyer feedback on AI appetite has been captured, and no call transcript documents the buyer's reaction to the Merlin narrative. The AI positioning is assumed strong based on product scope, but unvalidated. The workshop is the venue to demonstrate Merlin Intake live (AI-powered requisition routing, natural-language intake, spend-category prediction), quantify the ROI (hours saved per requisition, faster approval cycles, maverick spend reduction), and position Merlin as the AI-native layer Oracle ERP lacks. Until the workshop validates buyer AI appetite and locks in AI fit as a decision criterion, AI positioning strength is uncertain.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Customer expectations and decision criteria are largely unknown. The product scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin Intake) signals the buyer wants a full S2P platform with AI intake, but no BRD, RFP draft, or stated priorities have been shared. The upcoming workshop and Q3 RFP round will formalize expectations. Preliminary alignment is assumed (the buyer committed to a workshop and Horizon attendance), but fit cannot be validated until criteria are documented.

Only Dr. Hamidey Hamid (Group Chief Procurement and Vendor Development Officer) is a confirmed buyer-side contact role on this opportunity; the CFO/COO (likely Economic Buyer), IT (ERP integration owner), finance (ROI validation), and legal (contract review) are unmapped. The account contact list shows Hidaya Yahaya (COO & CFO) and Nani Tajuddin (Group Director, Secretarial & Legal) as high-value expansion targets. During the upcoming workshop, bring the broader evaluation committee into the room—co-present with the relevant stakeholder, meet the CFO/COO to validate budget authority, and add finance, IT, and legal contacts to the opportunity contact roles. A single-threaded deal is at risk if the Decision Maker changes role, goes silent, or cannot carry the business case to the Economic Buyer. Build redundancy now while engagement is warm.

deltas.to: Run the workshop as a value-discovery and champion-building session, not a product demo. The half-day workshop scheduled for Q3 is the single highest-leverage e
deltas.subject: Run the workshop as a value-discovery and champion-building session, not a product demo. The half-day workshop scheduled for Q3 is the single highest-leverage event before the RFP. Structure it to (a) quantify the business case—facilitate a pain-discovery exercise with procurement ops, finance, and supply chain to surface procurement spend baseline, maverick spend rate, contract approval cycle time, invoice-processing cost, and savings target, then map Zycus capabilities (Merlin Intake AI automation, iContract CLM, iSupplier collaboration, eProcurement user experience) to measurable ROI; (b) surface and lock in decision criteria before the RFP formalizes the scorecard—AI/Merlin fit, ERP integration (likely Oracle or SAP), user adoption, TCO, InfoSec/data residency for Malaysia; (c) introduce the CFO/COO Economic Buyer and secure budget sign-off in principle; (d) convert Dr. Hamidey from a willing procurement partner to an active champion by giving him the tools to advocate internally (business case deck, competitive positioning vs. Oracle, draft RFP language that favors Zycus strengths). A well-run workshop with the right stakeholders in the room can collapse 8 weeks of discovery into 4 hours and position Zycus as the preferred vendor before Oracle frames the RFP.

deltas.to: Build competitive intelligence on Oracle before the RFP round. Oracle is named in Competitors__c but the competitive context is a black box—no insight on whethe
deltas.subject: Build competitive intelligence on Oracle before the RFP round. Oracle is named in Competitors__c but the competitive context is a black box—no insight on whether Oracle means Oracle Procurement Cloud (the direct S2P peer), Oracle ERP Cloud (the incumbent ERP with procurement add-on modules), or a placeholder. During pre-workshop discovery calls and at the workshop itself, surface the competitive landscape: who else is being evaluated, is Oracle the incumbent ERP, what is the buyer's Oracle pricing and fit experience, what are the decision criteria Oracle cannot meet (likely AI-native intake automation, procurement-specific depth, faster time-to-value, and lower TCO vs. an ERP-bundle play). Use the workshop to position Zycus strengths (Merlin Intake as the AI layer Oracle lacks, iContract advanced CLM vs. Oracle's basic contract repository, healthcare-sector references) and lock in evaluation criteria that favor Zycus before Oracle shapes the RFP. A competitor named but uncharacterized is a latent threat; map it early.

value.name: Oracle
value.how_we_win: Position Merlin Intake as the AI-native requisition automation layer Oracle ERP lacks; emphasize procurement-specific depth (iContract advanced CLM, iSupplier collaboration, iSource strategic sourcing) vs. Oracle's ERP-add-on procurement modules; prove faster time-to-value and lower TCO with healthcare-sector references; lock in decision criteria around AI, user experience, and S2P completeness before Oracle frames the RFP as an ERP-bundle play.

value.detail: Economic Buyer (CFO/COO) is unmapped; budget authority and C-level sponsorship unconfirmed. Dr. Hamidey (Decision Maker, procurement process owner) is the single buyer-side contact role; Hidaya Yahaya (COO & CFO per account contact list) is the likely Economic Buyer but has not been engaged on this opportunity. A $200k S2P platform investment typically requires CFO/COO sign-off; without executive alignment, the deal can stall post-RFP at budget approval. The workshop is the opportunity to meet the CFO/COO, validate budget authority, and secure C-level sponsorship before the RFP formalizes the evaluation.
value.source: OpportunityContactRole query (single buyer-side contact), account contact list (Hidaya Yahaya COO & CFO unmapped on opp)

value.detail: Competitive intelligence on Oracle is thin; no insight on Oracle's role (incumbent ERP vs. shortlist challenger), pricing, buyer sentiment, or Oracle-specific weakness. Oracle is a credible enterprise brand and likely the incumbent ERP if Radicare runs Oracle financials; without competitive intelligence, Zycus cannot position the wedge (Merlin AI, procurement-specific depth, lower TCO) or lock in decision criteria that favor Zycus before Oracle frames the RFP. The workshop and pre-RFP discovery calls are the venues to surface the competitive landscape and build the Oracle counterstrategy.
value.source: Competitors__c (Oracle named, no detail), Next_Step__c (no competitive context)

value.detail: Workshop timing is vague ('to be set-up soon' per Next_Step__c 13 May 2026, now ~6 weeks ago); RFP expected Q3 2026 (Jul-Sep, 1-9 weeks away). If the workshop slips into late Q3 or the RFP launches before the workshop, Zycus loses the opportunity to surface decision criteria, quantify the business case, and position strengths before the RFP formalizes the evaluation. The next move is to lock the workshop date within the next 14 days and run it before the RFP drops.

A well-run workshop with the right stakeholders in the room can collapse 8 weeks of discovery into 4 hours and position Zycus as the preferred vendor before Oracle frames the RFP.
